1.synthesis: Paul announces his third visit: 'Every charge must be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses' (v.1). He warned those who sinned before and warns all the others now: when he comes he will not spare — they seek proof that Christ speaks in him; let them know that Christ is not weak in dealing with them but is powerful among them (vv.2–3). For he was crucified in weakness but lives by the power of God; and those who are in him are weak in him but will live with him by God's power toward the Corinthians (v.4). He turns the examination back on the Corinthians: 'Examine yourselves, whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves' (v.5). He hopes they will recognize that he has not failed the test; and he prays they will do no wrong — not to prove himself but so that they may do what is right, even if he appears to have failed (v.7). He cannot do anything against the truth but only for it (v.8).
1.historical_context: The citation of Deuteronomy 19:15 ('two or three witnesses') in v.1 establishes the coming visit within the legal framework for judicial proceedings in Israel: Paul's third coming to Corinth will function as the third witness, sufficient to establish charges and proceed with disciplinary action. Chrysostom observes the pattern of Paul's pastoral patience: 'being so abundant and vehement in his admonitions, but so tardy and procrastinating in his punishments. For he did not chastise them immediately on their sinning, but warned them once and again; and not even so, upon their paying no attention, does he exact punishment, but warns again.' Two written warnings precede the anticipated third in-person warning-and-action. The self-examination Paul calls for in v.5 was a recognized practice in Hellenistic moral philosophy (Socratic self-examination) but Paul gives it explicitly christological content: the criterion is whether 'Jesus Christ is in you.'
1.literary_structure: The chapter moves from warning to self-examination to aspiration to authority-statement. The christological center is v.4: crucified in weakness / lives by God's power — the fundamental pattern that governs both apostolic ministry and the community's own life. The self-examination of v.5 is not an act of introspective anxiety but a pastoral inversion: you demand proof of Christ in me — look for proof of Christ in yourselves, and if you find it you will have also found confirmation of my ministry. The prayer of v.7 — that the Corinthians do what is right even if Paul appears to have failed — is the climactic statement of apostolic unselfishness: he would rather appear disqualified and see them flourish than appear powerful and see them fail.
1.theological_application: Chrysostom praises Paul's measured approach: the 'abundant admonitions' before punishment are an expression of pastoral wisdom — the goal is restoration, not retribution, and every warning given is another opportunity for the Corinthians to change course before the disciplinary action becomes necessary. That Paul threatens in order to make the threat unnecessary is not weakness but the logic of pastoral love. Gill on v.5 traces what self-examination tests: 'whether they were in the doctrine of faith, having a spiritual and experimental knowledge of it, true love and affection for it, an hearty belief of it, having felt the power of it upon their hearts; or whether they were in the exercise of the grace of faith, in the lively, active, vigorous exercise of that grace.' The test is not intellectual assent but living, felt, grace-produced faith. The warning of verse 5's close — 'unless indeed you fail the test' — is not a threat but a sober possibility: communities can lose their christological center while maintaining their religious forms. Gill on v.8: Paul 'could do nothing against the truth' — his whole ministry was constituted by and ordered toward the truth of the gospel, so that any action that served his personal authority at the truth's expense would be self-defeating.
1.christology: 'He was crucified in weakness but lives by the power of God' (v.4) is the letter's final and fullest statement of the cruciform logic. Christ's weakness and power are not sequential contradictions but the single pattern of his saving work: the cross was the extreme of weakness, the resurrection the supreme manifestation of power — and both are permanent features of his reign. Those in him are 'weak in him' — sharing his crucified pattern — but 'will live with him by God's power toward you' — sharing his resurrection life directed toward the Corinthians' benefit. The christological argument is: if you find Christ in yourselves (v.5), you have already answered your own demand for proof of Christ in Paul — for the same Christ who works in them worked through Paul to plant the gospel among them in the first place.

11.synthesis: Paul closes with a cluster of brief imperatives: 'Rejoice. Aim for restoration. Comfort one another. Agree with one another. Live in peace' (v.11). The promise follows: the God of love and peace will be with them. They are to greet one another with a holy kiss; all the saints greet them (vv.12–13). The letter closes with the Trinitarian benediction: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all' (v.14).
11.historical_context: The closing imperatives of v.11 are all present tense: these are the ongoing postures of the community, not one-off actions. The 'holy kiss' (philēma hagion) was the liturgical greeting exchanged in early Christian worship — attested in multiple Pauline letters and in Justin Martyr's second-century description of the Sunday gathering. It signified the genuine familial bond of the community and was 'holy' because it was conducted in the Spirit's presence and under the new covenant's framework of reconciliation. The Trinitarian structure of v.14 is the most explicit three-person formula in Paul's letters, predating the formal Trinitarian theology of the councils but providing one of its key scriptural foundations.
11.literary_structure: The five imperatives of v.11 compress the letter's practical agenda into a single verse: the restoration Paul has been seeking throughout the letter (rejoice, be restored, agree, be at peace). The promise — God of love and peace will be with you — grounds the imperatives not in the Corinthians' capacity but in the divine presence. The Trinitarian benediction of v.14 distributes the three divine gifts to the three persons: grace belongs distinctively to the Lord Jesus Christ (the mediator of grace); love belongs distinctively to God the Father (the source of the sending); fellowship belongs distinctively to the Holy Spirit (the bond of union between believers and God). The three-fold structure mirrors the letter's argument: grace accomplished by Christ, love demonstrated in the cross by the Father, fellowship sustained by the Spirit.
11.theological_application: Gill on v.14 expands the meaning of 'the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ': 'either the love of Christ … This is the ground and foundation of all he has done and underwent for his people; of his becoming their surety; of his incarnation, obedience, sufferings, and death in their room and stead … or else by the grace of Christ is meant the fulness of grace that is in him, which he has laid up for his people in himself.' The grace Paul prays for is not a disposition of kindness but the whole economy of what Christ is and has done — his fullness given to his people for their ongoing life. Gill on v.11 reads 'rejoice' as specifically spiritual joy: 'in Christ, their Saviour and Redeemer; in his person, in whom they were accepted; in his righteousness, by which they were justified; in his blood, by which they were washed and cleansed; and in his fulness, from which they were supplied.' The five imperatives are not moral exhortations toward community improvement but descriptions of what life looks like when the Trinitarian gift of v.14 is actually received and inhabited.
11.christology: The Trinitarian benediction places the Lord Jesus Christ first — 'the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ' — in a letter that has consistently argued that grace, power, reconciliation, and ministry all flow from Christ crucified and risen. The final word of the letter is thus the same as its first theological move: the grace of Christ is the beginning and end of everything Paul has commended and commanded. The fellowship of the Holy Spirit (koinōnia tou hagiou pneumatos) is the bond by which the grace of Christ and the love of the Father reach the Corinthians in their actual experience — the Spirit is not added as a third item alongside the others but as the one through whom the other two gifts are mediated and received. The benediction is simultaneously a prayer and a promise: may these three gifts be with you all — and they will be, because the God of love and peace has promised to be present wherever his people gather in the name of Christ.
